Name: rate_distortion_quantization_model
Title: Navigating the Compression Landscape: An Information-Theoretic Approach to Model Quantization
Short Hypothesis: By framing model quantization as a rate-distortion problem, we can derive novel quantization schemes that approach the theoretical limits of compression while minimizing task-relevant distortion.
Related Work: Existing work has applied rate-distortion theory to model compression, notably in 'Rate Distortion For Model Compression: From Theory To Practice' and 'Radio: Rate-Distortion Optimization for Large Language Model Compression'. However, they often focus on empirical results without providing a theoretical framework for quantization strategies. Our proposal distinguishes itself by introducing a principled method for deriving quantization schemes directly from the rate-distortion function, allowing us to explore beyond existing empirical methods.
Abstract: As deep learning models grow in size and complexity, their deployment in resource-constrained environments necessitates efficient compression techniques. This proposal seeks to investigate model quantization through the lens of rate-distortion theory, providing a rigorous foundation for deriving optimal quantization strategies. We will explore the relationship between the representation capacity of neural networks and the associated rate-distortion trade-offs. By developing a set of quantization algorithms grounded in information theory, we aim to identify achievable rates that minimize task-relevant distortion. Our approach will involve deriving bounds on the compression efficiency of various quantization strategies and validating them through a series of experiments designed to measure distortion against established theoretical limits. The outcomes of this research will provide deeper insights into the fundamental limits of model compression and establish a clearer path towards more efficient quantization methods that are both theoretically sound and practically applicable.
Experiments: 1. Develop a range of quantization algorithms based on derived rate-distortion functions and compare them against traditional quantization methods.
2. Evaluate the performance of these algorithms on benchmark datasets, measuring accuracy and model performance metrics post-quantization.
3. Conduct experiments to derive empirical distortion-rate curves for the proposed quantization schemes and compare them with theoretical bounds.
4. Assess the impact of varying task relevance on quantization effectiveness by manipulating the input data distributions and observing the resulting distortion.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The primary risk involves the potential complexity of deriving effective quantization strategies that are computationally feasible in practice. Additionally, while the theoretical framework is robust, translating these ideas into practical algorithms may present challenges. There is also a risk that empirical results may not align with theoretical predictions, necessitating further adjustments to the proposed methods.
